note: Core verified claims for script anchoring. R2 sensitivity: state only adjudicated facts; attribute trial assertions to prosecution/testimony. Dollar figures to be re-confirmed against court record before script lock.

claims.normalized_claim: On November 2, 2023, a federal jury in the Southern District of New York found Samuel Bankman-Fried, founder of the crypto exchange FTX and trading firm Alameda Research, guilty on all seven counts: two counts of wire fraud, two counts of conspiracy to commit wire fraud, one count of conspiracy to commit securities fraud, one count of conspiracy to commit commodities fraud, and one count of conspiracy to commit money laundering.
allowed_wording: A New York jury found him guilty on all seven counts.
He was convicted of fraud and conspiracy.
prohibited_wording: He admitted to stealing the money.
He was the only one responsible.
claims.notes: Adjudicated fact. The conviction (not allegation) is what we state as fact.

claims.normalized_claim: Prosecutors and multiple major outlets described the FTX fraud as one of the largest financial frauds in US history, drawing comparison to Bernie Madoff's Ponzi scheme; Bankman-Fried faced a statutory maximum of up to 115 years.
allowed_wording: prosecutors called it one of the biggest financial frauds in American history
claims.notes: Attribute the 'largest' framing to prosecutors/outlets, not as our own absolute claim.

claims.normalized_claim: On March 28, 2024, Judge Lewis Kaplan sentenced Bankman-Fried to 25 years in federal prison and ordered forfeiture of approximately $11.02 billion.
allowed_wording: He was sentenced to 25 years in prison.
The judge ordered more than $11 billion in forfeiture.

claims.normalized_claim: Reported losses associated with the collapse include roughly $8 billion owed to FTX customers, about $1.7 billion to FTX equity investors, and about $1.3 billion to Alameda Research lenders.
allowed_wording: roughly $8 billion of customer money was gone
claims.notes: Re-confirm against sentencing record / bankruptcy filings before script lock; use 'about/roughly' framing. Note later recoveries in bankruptcy mean creditors were eventually repaid a large share — mention to stay accurate.

claims.normalized_claim: At trial, FTX co-founder Gary Wang testified that, on Bankman-Fried's instruction, FTX's code gave Alameda Research special privileges — including an 'allow_negative' flag and an effectively enormous line of credit — letting Alameda withdraw and spend customer deposits in ways ordinary customers could not.
allowed_wording: A co-founder testified that the code let Alameda spend customer money.
Prosecutors pointed to a special setting in the code that gave Alameda a near-unlimited line of credit.
prohibited_wording: Everyone at FTX knew and approved.
The code automatically stole money on its own.
claims.notes: Attribute to trial testimony (Wang). This is the 'hidden mechanism' spine of the episode.

claims.normalized_claim: The special Alameda credit line was reported at trial/coverage as reaching figures on the order of tens of billions of dollars of borrowing capacity, far beyond any normal customer.
allowed_wording: a line of credit far larger than any ordinary user's
claims.notes: The widely-cited '$65B' is a credit-LIMIT/capacity figure, not the amount stolen. Do NOT conflate with the ~$8B customer shortfall. Confirm phrasing carefully before script lock.

claims.normalized_claim: Bankman-Fried's estimated personal fortune peaked around $26 billion in early 2022; during the November 2022 collapse Bloomberg recorded his assets falling from about $16 billion to under $1 billion in a single day (Nov 8, 2022), and to effectively zero by the FTX bankruptcy filing on Nov 11, 2022.
allowed_wording: an estimated fortune of around twenty-six billion dollars
by some estimates his wealth fell from sixteen billion to near zero in a single day
prohibited_wording: he had exactly $26 billion in cash
claims.notes: These are estimates of paper net worth; use 'estimated/by some estimates'. Vivid scale beat.

claims.normalized_claim: At its peak FTX ran a high-profile marketing push including a 2022 Super Bowl commercial starring Larry David and the naming rights to the Miami Heat's arena ('FTX Arena', a deal approved in 2021); after the collapse the arena name was stripped and later became the Kaseya Center (2023).
allowed_wording: its name on an NBA arena
prohibited_wording: the celebrities were in on the fraud
claims.notes: Do NOT imply endorsers knew of or took part in wrongdoing. Use to show the trusted public image.

claims.normalized_claim: Bankman-Fried cultivated an 'eccentric genius' image — rumpled T-shirt and shorts, sleeping on beanbag chairs — and was reported (by FTX's head of product) to have played the video game League of Legends during an investor pitch meeting.
allowed_wording: he was reported to have played a video game during an investor pitch
prohibited_wording: he never cared about the company
claims.notes: Characterization detail; attribute the gaming anecdote ('reportedly'). Color, not a fact-critical claim.
